message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Several SARS-CoV-2 vaccine candidates are currently in the pipeline. This study aims to inform SARS-CoV-2 vaccine development, licensure, decision-making, and implementation by determining key preferred vaccine product characteristics and associated population-level impact.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Vaccination impact was assessed at various efficacies using an age-structured mathematical model describing SARS-CoV-2 transmission and disease progression, with application for China.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    A prophylactic vaccine with efficacy against acquisition (
                    <jats:italic>VE</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    ) of ≥70% is needed to eliminate this infection. A vaccine with
                    <jats:italic>VE</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    <70% will still have a major impact, and may control the infection if it reduces infectiousness or infection duration among those vaccinated who acquire the infection, or alternatively if supplemented with a moderate social-distancing intervention (<20% reduction in contact rate), or complemented with herd immunity. Vaccination is cost-effective. For a vaccine with
                    <jats:italic>VE</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    of 50%, number of vaccinations needed to avert one infection is only 2.4, one severe disease case is 25.5, one critical disease case is 33.2, and one death is 65.1. Gains in effectiveness are achieved by initially prioritizing those ≥60 years. Probability of a major outbreak is virtually zero with a vaccine with
                    <jats:italic>VE</jats:italic>
                    <jats:sub>
                      <jats:italic>S</jats:italic>
                    </jats:sub>
                    ≥70%, regardless of number of virus introductions. Yet, an increase in social contact rate among those vaccinated (behavior compensation) can undermine vaccine impact.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Even a partially-efficacious vaccine can offer a fundamental solution to control SARS-CoV-2 infection and at high cost-effectiveness. In addition to the primary endpoint on infection acquisition, developers should assess natural history and disease progression outcomes and/or proxy biomarkers, since such secondary endpoints may prove critical in licensure, decision-making, and vaccine impact.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Epidemiological impact of SARS-CoV-2 vaccination: mathematical modeling analyses
